Spring-Grid Frequency Response Report (10x10, STLS Full + ROM)

Setup

  • Fine grid: 10x10 with 90 free DOFs (top row clamped).
  • ROM: block size 2, coarse grid 5x5, 25 DOFs.
  • Both regressed operators are fit by locality-masked STLS on the linear regression problem a + C v - f = -K u, using the same multi-frequency bottom-row training forcing as the rom-vs-full report.
  • Frequency response forcing is held fixed at unit amplitude on every bottom-row free DOF (in phase). The ROM forcing is the block-average restriction of this fine-grid forcing.
  • Mass: M = I. Damping: Rayleigh, C = alpha M + beta K_phys with alpha = 0.05, beta = 0.02. The same physical damping is paired with each predicted K when computing its response (damping is treated as known).
  • Measurement noise on training data: none (clean data).
  • Steady-state response is computed analytically by solving (K - omega^2 M + i omega C) U = F0 at each omega.

Operator matrices

True vs learned stiffness operators on both grids. The top row shows the fine-grid K_free (90×90); the bottom row shows the projected coarse operator K_rom = R_avg K_free P_block and the learned ROM operator. Identical color scales per row make differences between true and learned matrices easy to spot.

Training forcing

Each training trajectory is driven by a localized sinusoidal point load on the bottom row of the lattice, with the spatial profile a Gaussian on the column index $j$ and the temporal profile a sine wave at trajectory-specific frequency and phase:

$f_k(t,\, x_j) \;=\; A \,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(j - j_{c,k})^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)\, \sin(\omega_k\, t + \phi_k), \qquad i = 0,\;\; j = 0,\dots,n-1$

with $\omega_k = \omega_{\mathrm{base}}\,\rho_k$, $\rho_k\in\{0.7,\,1.0,\,1.3,\,1.7,\,2.1,\,2.7,\,3.3,\,4.1\}$, phase $\phi_k = \pi k / N_{\mathrm{traj}}$, center column $j_{c,k}$ varied symmetrically around $(n-1)/2$, and amplitude $A$ and width $\sigma$ held fixed across trajectories. The concrete values used for this run are:

Trajectory$\omega_k$ [rad/s]$f_k$ [Hz]$j_c$$\phi_k$ [rad]$A$$\sigma$
04.39820.70004.500.00001.001.50
16.28321.00003.000.78541.001.50
28.16811.30006.001.57081.001.50
310.68141.70002.002.35621.001.50

Heatmaps below show $f_k(t, x_j)$ on the bottom row over the training window for each trajectory.

Training rollout: true vs Full vs lifted ROM

One-row, three-panel quiver animation of trajectory 0 from the training set: the data ($u(t)$ on the fine grid), the Full STLS rollout under identical forcing, and the ROM STLS rollout (lifted to the fine grid via $P_{\mathrm{block}}$). This is the time-domain analogue of the frequency-response comparison below.

Training rollout: true vs Full vs lifted ROM

Response curves

Three amplitude metrics over the same omega sweep. L2 is the Euclidean norm of |U(omega)| over all 90 fine free DOFs (ROM is lifted to fine via block-constant prolongation before measurement); max is the peak DOF amplitude; top-near is the mean amplitude on the row just below the clamped top. Vertical guides mark the true natural frequencies sqrt(eig(K_true)).

Reading the curves: the True model is plotted as a wide solid black line. The Regressed Full overlays it as a dashed blue line, and the Regressed ROM is a solid red line. When the Full regression converges to the true operator, the dashed blue line lies directly on top of the black True line. Quantitative deviation is shown in the dedicated error figure below.

Headline: L2 response with eigenfrequency overlay

Response error vs omega

For each amplitude metric, |metric_pred(omega) - metric_true(omega)| for the Full and ROM regressed models.

Per-DOF amplitude at the resonance peak

Steady-state displacement magnitude |U_j(omega_peak)| on the fine grid for each of the three models. omega_peak = 0.234 (argmax of true L2 response).

Per-DOF phase at the resonance peak

Selected animation frequencies

ClassomegaTrue L2 amplitude
small2.79657.4649e-01
medium0.02662.7280e+01
big0.23441.2489e+02

Steady-state animations

For each of the three selected frequencies, the steady-state response u(t) = Re(U(omega) e^(i omega t)) is rendered for two periods of oscillation. Quiver arrows show the scalar deflection field as a vertical out-of-plane component on the lattice; heatmaps show |u(x, t)|. The "lifted ROM" frames are block-constant on each 2x2 block by construction of the prolongation operator.
